qa_pairs.question: How do you migrate a volume-backed instance to the New Arbutus Cloud using Glance images?
qa_pairs.answer: First, on the Legacy Arbutus instance, you need to shut down the instance and detach the volume. If it's a boot volume, delete the instance but keep the volume. Then, create an image of the desired volume using the OpenStack CLI command `openstack image create –volume <volume name/id> <newimagename> --private`. Once the image is active, download it from the Legacy Arbutus session using `openstack image save --file <filename> <image-id>`. Next, in a New Arbutus Cloud session on the migration host, upload the image using `openstack --os-cloud <cloud> image create --private --file <file> <newImageName>`. Finally, in the New Arbutus Cloud web UI, navigate to Compute -> Images, select your uploaded image, and choose 'Create Volume' from the drop-down menu to create a new volume which can then be attached to instances or used to boot a new instance.

qa_pairs.question: What is an alternative method for migrating a volume-backed instance using the Linux 'dd' utility?
qa_pairs.answer: An alternative method involves launching a temporary migration host (e.g., p1-1.5gb) on Legacy Arbutus and installing the OpenStack CLI on it. After installing the CLI, you delete the instance you intend to migrate (but do not delete its attached volume). Attach this volume to the temporary migration host. Then, use the `dd` utility piped to the OpenStack CLI to create an image directly from the attached volume and upload it to the New Arbutus Cloud, for example: `dd if=/dev/vdb | openstack image create --private --container-format bare --disk-format raw "volumemigrate"`. This image will then be visible in the New Arbutus Cloud web UI under Compute -> Images, from which you can launch new instances.

qa_pairs.question: How are large volumes (over 150 GB) migrated from Legacy Arbutus to New Arbutus using the Linux 'dd' command?
qa_pairs.answer: For large volumes, you need to create temporary instances on both the Legacy Arbutus and New Arbutus Clouds, and assign them floating IP addresses for SSH access. On the temporary Legacy Arbutus instance, install `epel-release`, `pv`, and `screen`. On the temporary New Arbutus instance, set `chmod u+s /bin/dd`. You must also copy your SSH private key from the New Arbutus temporary instance to the Legacy Arbutus temporary instance and ensure SSH security rules permit connections between them. For each volume to be moved, create an empty volume of the same size on New Arbutus (marking it bootable if necessary) and attach it to the New Arbutus temporary instance. Attach the source volume from Legacy Arbutus to its temporary instance. Finally, on the Legacy Arbutus temporary instance, use a command within a `screen` session like `sudo dd bs=16M if=/dev/vdb | pv -s 96G | ssh -i key.pem user@xxx.xx.xx.xx "sudo dd bs=16M of=/dev/vdb"` to stream an exact copy of the volume over SSH to the new volume.
